explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{d x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} \\ = & \int\left(\frac{1}{\cos x}-\frac{1}{1+\cos x}\right) d x \\ = & \int\left(\sec x-\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}}\right) d x \\ = & \int\left(\sec x-\frac{1}{2} \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2}\right) d x \\ = & \log |\sec x+\tan x|-\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}a & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & b & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & c\end{array}\right]$ where $a=7^x, b=7^{7^x}, c=7^{7^{7^x}}$ then $\int|A| d x$, (Where $|A|$ is the determinant of the matrix $A$ ) is equal to

options.content: $\frac{7^{7^x}}{(\log 7)^3}+\mathrm{k}$, where k is constant of integration
$\frac{7^{7^{7^x}}}{\log 7}+\mathrm{k}$, where k is constant of integration
$\frac{7^{7^{7^x}}}{(\log 7)^3}+\mathrm{k}$, where k is constant of integration
$7^{7^{7^x}}(\log 7)^3+\mathrm{k}$, where k is constant of integration

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & |\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \mathrm{~b} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \mathrm{c} \end{array}\right|=\mathrm{abc} \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int|\mathrm{A}| \mathrm{d} x=\int 7^x 7^{7^x} 7^{7^{7^x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } 7^{7^{7^x}}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow 7^{7^{7^x}}(\log 7)^3 7^{7^x} 7^x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow 7^{7^x} 7^x \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{7^{7^x}(\log 7)^3}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}(\log 7)^3} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{(\log 7)^3} \int \mathrm{dt}=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{(\log 7)^3}+\mathrm{k} \\ & =\frac{7^{7^x}}{(\log 7)^3}+\mathrm{k} \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\sin 7 x}{\cos 9 x \cos 2 x} d x=\int \frac{\sin (9 x-2 x)}{\cos 9 x \cos 2 x} d x \\ & =\int \frac{(\sin 9 x \cos 2 x-\cos 9 x \sin 2 x)}{\cos 9 x \cos 2 x} d x \\ & =\int(\tan 9 x-\tan 2 x) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{9} \log |\sec (9 x)|-\frac{1}{2} \log |\sec (2 x)|+c \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sin ^2 x \cos ^2 x} \\ & =\int \frac{\left(\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x\right)}{\cos ^2 x \sin ^2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int\left(\frac{1}{\cos ^2 x}+\frac{1}{\sin ^2 x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x+\int \operatorname{cosec}^2 x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\tan x-\cot x+c \end{aligned} $$

content:
$$ \int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x^{\frac{1}{2}}+x^{\frac{1}{3}}}=\mathrm{A} x^{\frac{1}{2}}+\mathrm{B} x^{\frac{1}{3}}+\mathrm{C} x^{\frac{1}{6}}+\mathrm{D} \log \left(x^{\frac{1}{6}}+1\right)+\mathrm{k} $$

(where k is the integration constant) then values of $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D are respectively,
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x^{\frac{1}{2}}+x^{\frac{1}{3}}} \\ & \text { Put } x^{\frac{1}{6}}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{t}^6 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d} x=6 \mathrm{t}^5 \mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{6 \mathrm{t}^5}{\mathrm{t}^3+\mathrm{t}^2} \mathrm{dt} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{6 t^5}{t^2(t+1)} d t=6 \int \frac{t^3}{t+1} d t \\ & =6 \int \frac{t^3+1-1}{t+1} d t=6 \int\left(t^2-t+1-\frac{1}{t+1}\right) d t \\ & =6\left[\frac{t^3}{3}-\frac{t^2}{2}+t-\log |t+1|\right]+k \\ & =2 \sqrt{x}-3 \sqrt[3]{x}+6 \sqrt[6]{x}-6 \log |\sqrt[6]{x}+1|+k \\ \therefore \quad A & =2, B=-3, C=6, D=-6 \end{aligned} $$

$x^2 \sin x+2 x \cos x+2 \sin x+\mathrm{c}$, where $c$ is the constant of integration

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int x^2 \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =x^2 \int \cos x \mathrm{~d} x-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(x^2\right) \int \cos x \mathrm{~d} x\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =x^2 \sin x-\int 2 x \sin x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =x^2 \sin x-2\left[x(-\cos x)-\int 1 \cdot(-\cos x) \mathrm{d} x\right] \\ & =x^2 \sin x-2(-x \cos x+\sin x)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =x^2 \sin x+2 x \cos x-2 \sin x+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\int \frac{\left(x^4+1\right)}{x\left(x^2+1\right)^2} d x=A \log |x|+\frac{B}{1+x^2}+c$, then $\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}$ is (where c is the constant of integration)
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int \frac{x^4+1}{x\left(x^2+1\right)^2} d x \\ & =\int \frac{x^4+1+2 x^2-2 x^2}{x\left(x^2+1\right)^2} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\left(x^2+1\right)^2-2 x^2}{x\left(x^2+1\right)^2} d x \\ & =\int \frac{1}{x} d x-\int \frac{2 x}{\left(x^2+1\right)^2} d x \end{aligned} $$

In second integral, put $x^2+1=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow 2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x-\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^2} \\ & =\log |x|+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\log |x|+\frac{1}{1+x^2}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

On comparing with the given expression, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & A=1, B=1 \\ \therefore & A-B=0 \end{array} $$

content: If $\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x^4+5 x^2+4}=\mathrm{A} \tan ^{-1} x+\mathrm{B} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+\mathrm{c}$ where c is a constant of integration, then

options.content: $\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{1}{4}$
$\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{3}, \mathrm{~B}=-\frac{1}{6}$
$\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{3}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{1}{6}$
$\quad \mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{~B}=-\frac{1}{4}$

explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } I=\int \frac{d x}{x^4+5 x^2+4} \\ \therefore \quad & I=\int \frac{1}{\left(x^2+1\right)\left(x^2+4\right)} d x \\ & \frac{1}{\left(x^2+1\right)\left(x^2+4\right)}=\frac{A}{x^2+1}+\frac{B}{x^2+4} \\ & \Rightarrow 1=A\left(x^2+4\right)+B\left(x^2+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 1=(A+B) x^2+4 A+B \\ & \Rightarrow A+B=0 \text { and } 4 A+B=0 \end{array} $$

On solving, we get $\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{3}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{-1}{3}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x^2+1} d x-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x^2+4} d x \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{6} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c \end{aligned} $$

On comparing with the given expression, we get $A=\frac{1}{3}, B=\frac{-1}{6}$

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \int \frac{\sqrt{\tan x}}{\sin x \cos x} d x & =\int \frac{\tan x}{\sqrt{\tan x} \sin x \cos x} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\sin x \sec x}{\sqrt{\tan x} \sin x \cos x} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\sec ^2 x}{\sqrt{\tan x}} d x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \int \frac{\sqrt{\tan x}}{\sin x \cos x} \mathrm{~d} x= & 2 \sqrt{\tan x}+c \\ & \ldots\left[\because \int \frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{\sqrt{f(x)}} \mathrm{d} x=2 \sqrt{f(x)}+c\right] \end{aligned} $$

options.content: $\frac{7}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}\left(\frac{x-\mathrm{b}}{x+\mathrm{a}}\right)^{\frac{9}{7}}+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration.
$\frac{7}{a+b}\left(\frac{x-b}{x+a}\right)^{\frac{5}{7}}+c$, where $c$ is the constant of integration.
$\frac{7}{2(a+b)}\left(\frac{x-b}{x+a}\right)^{\frac{2}{7}}+c$, where $c$ is the constant of integration.
$\frac{7}{a+b}\left(\frac{x-b}{x+a}\right)^{\frac{1}{7}}+c$, where $c$ is the constant of integration.

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Let } \mathrm{I} & =\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{(x+\mathrm{a})^{\frac{9}{7}}(x-\mathrm{b})^{\frac{5}{7}}} \\ & =\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{(x+\mathrm{a})^2\left(\frac{x-\mathrm{b}}{x+\mathrm{a}}\right)^{\frac{5}{7}}} \\ \text { Put } \frac{x-\mathrm{b}}{x+\mathrm{a}} & =\mathrm{t} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{a+b}{(x+a)^2} d x=d t \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{a+b} \int \frac{1}{t^{\frac{5}{7}}} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{a+b} \cdot \frac{t^{\frac{2}{7}}}{\frac{2}{7}}+c \\ & =\frac{7}{2(a+b)}\left(\frac{x-b}{x+a}\right)^{\frac{2}{7}}+c \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int \frac{d x}{x\left(x^3+1\right)} \\ & =\int \frac{x^2}{x^3\left(x^3+1\right)} d x \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \text { Put } & x^3=t \Rightarrow 3 x^2 d x=d t \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t(t+1)} \\ = & \frac{1}{3} \int\left(\frac{1}{t}-\frac{1}{t+1}\right) d t \\ = & \frac{1}{3}(\log |t|-\log |t+1|)+c \\ = & \frac{1}{3} \log \left|\frac{t}{t+1}\right|+c \\ = & \log \left(\sqrt[3]{\frac{x^3}{x^3+1}}\right)+c \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{x^4 \cos \left(\tan ^{-1} x^5\right)}{1+x^{10}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } \tan ^{-1}\left(x^5\right)=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+\left(x^5\right)^2} \cdot 5 x^4 \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5 x^4 \mathrm{~d} x}{1+x^{10}}=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{5} \int \cos \mathrm{tdt} \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \sin \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \sin \left(\tan ^{-1} x^5\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \int \mathrm{e}^x\left(\frac{x+5}{(x+6)^2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int \mathrm{e}^x\left(\frac{x+6-1}{(x+6)^2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int \mathrm{e}^x\left[\frac{1}{x+6}-\frac{1}{(x+6)^2}\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{e}^x}{x+6}+c \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad \ldots\left[\because \int \mathrm{e}^x\left[\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{c}\right] \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \quad \text { Let } I=\int \frac{d x}{3 \cos 2 x+5} \\ & \quad \text { Put } \tan x=t \\ & \therefore \quad x=\tan ^{-1} t \\ & \therefore \quad d x=\frac{d t}{1+t^2} \text { and } \cos 2 x=\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2} \\ & \therefore \quad I=\int \frac{\frac{d t}{1+t^2}}{3\left(\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}\right)+5} \\ & \quad=\int \frac{d t}{8+2 t^2} \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{4+t^2} \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)+c \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x}{2}\right)+c \end{aligned} $$

options.content: $(\log 5 \sin \theta-2)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$5 \log (5 \sin \theta-2)-\frac{8}{(\sin \theta-2)}+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log (5 \sin \theta-2)+\frac{8}{(\sin \theta-2)}+c$, where $c$ is the constant of integration
$\log (5 \sin \theta-2)+\frac{1}{(\sin \theta-2)}+c$, where $c$ is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let\,\, I & =\int \frac{(5 \sin \theta-2) \cos \theta}{5-\cos ^2 \theta-4 \sin \theta} d \theta \\ & =\int \frac{(5 \sin \theta-2) \cos \theta}{5-\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)-4 \sin \theta} d \theta \\ & =\int \frac{(5 \sin \theta-2) \cos \theta}{4+\sin ^2 \theta-4 \sin \theta} d \theta \end{aligned} $$

Let $\sin \theta=t \Rightarrow \cos \theta d \theta=d t$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore I & =\int \frac{5 t-2}{t^2-4 t+4} d t \\ & =\int \frac{5 t-2}{(t-2)^2} d t \end{aligned} $$

Let $\frac{5 t-2}{(t-2)^2}=\frac{A}{t-2}+\frac{B}{(t-2)^2}$

On solving we get, $\mathrm{A}=5$ and $\mathrm{B}=8$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{5}{t-2} d t+\int \frac{8}{(t-2)^2} d t \\ & =5 \log |t-2|-\frac{8}{t-2}+c \\ & =5 \log (\sin \theta-2)-\frac{8}{\sin \theta-2}+c \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \text { } \begin{aligned} Let\,\,\, \mathrm{I} & =\int \sqrt{x^2+3 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \sqrt{x^2+3 x+\frac{9}{4}-\frac{9}{4}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \sqrt{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}}{2} \sqrt{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2} \\ & -\frac{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2}{2} \log \left(x+\frac{3}{2}+\sqrt{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{2 x+3}{4} \sqrt{x^2+3 x}-\frac{9}{8} \log \left(x+\frac{3}{2}+\sqrt{x^2+3 x}\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sqrt{x}+x} \\ & \quad=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sqrt{x}(1+\sqrt{x})} \\ & \quad \text { Put } 1+\sqrt{x}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \quad \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=2 \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}} \\ & \quad=2 \log |\mathrm{t}|+\mathrm{c} \\ & \quad=2 \log |1+\sqrt{x}|+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

explanation:
$$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let \,\,\,\, I & =\int\left(\frac{x-3}{x^2+9}\right)^2 d x \\ & =\int \frac{x^2-6 x+9}{\left(x^2+9\right)^2} d x \\ & =\int\left[\frac{1}{x^2+9}-\frac{6 x}{\left(x^2+9\right)^2}\right] d x \\ & =\int \frac{1}{x^2+3^2} d x-3 \int \frac{2 x}{\left(x^2+9\right)^2} d x \end{aligned} $$

In $2^{\text {nd }}$ integral,

Put $x^2+9=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow 2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$

content: If $\int \frac{2 x^2+3}{\left(x^2-1\right)\left(x^2-4\right)} \mathrm{d} x=\log \left[\left(\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right)^{\mathrm{a}} \cdot\left(\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right)^{\mathrm{b}}\right]+\mathrm{c}$, (where c is the constant of integration) then the value of $a+b$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $\frac{2 x^2+3}{\left(x^2-4\right)\left(x^2-1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{x^2-4}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{x^2-1}$

$$ \therefore \quad 2 x^2+3=\mathrm{A}\left(x^2-1\right)+\mathrm{B}\left(x^2-4\right) $$

Comparing the coefficient of $x^2$ and constant term on both sides, we get

$$ A+B=2 \text { and }-A-4 B=3 $$

Solving these two equations, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & A=\frac{11}{3}, B=\frac{-5}{3} \\ \therefore & \int \frac{2 x^2+3}{\left(x^2-4\right)\left(x^2-1\right)} d x \\ & =\int\left(\frac{\frac{11}{3}}{x^2-4}-\frac{\frac{5}{3}}{x^2-1}\right) d x \\ & =\frac{11}{3 \times 2 \times 2} \log \left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|-\frac{5}{6} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right|+c \\ & =\log \left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|^{11}+\log \left|\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right|^{\frac{5}{6}}+c \\ & =\log \left[\left(\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right)^{\frac{11}{12}}\left(\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right)^{\frac{5}{6}}\right]+c \\ \therefore & a=\frac{11}{12} \text { and } b=\frac{5}{6} \\ & a+b=\frac{11}{12}+\frac{5}{6}=\frac{21}{12} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\quad \int \frac{3 \sin x \cos x}{4 \sin x+7} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{A} \sin x-\mathrm{Blog}(4 \sin x+7)+\mathrm{c}$ where c is the constant of integration, then the value of $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{3 \sin x \cdot \cos x}{4 \sin x+7} \mathrm{~d} x$

Put $4 \sin x+7=\mathrm{u} \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{\mathrm{u}-7}{4}$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 4 \cos x d x=d u \Rightarrow \cos x d x=\frac{d u}{4} \\ \therefore & I=\int \frac{3\left(\frac{u-7}{4}\right) \cdot \frac{d u}{4}}{u} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{3}{16} \int \frac{\mathrm{u}-7}{\mathrm{u}} \mathrm{du} \\ & =\frac{3}{16} \int\left(1-\frac{7}{\mathrm{u}}\right) \mathrm{du} \\ & =\frac{3}{16}(\mathrm{u}-7 \log \mathrm{u})+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & =\frac{3}{16} \mathrm{u}-\frac{21}{16} \log \mathrm{u}+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & =\frac{3}{16}(4 \sin x+7)-\frac{21}{16} \log (4 \sin x+7)+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & =\frac{12}{16} \sin x+\frac{21}{16}-\frac{21}{16} \log (4 \sin x+7)+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & =\frac{3}{4} \sin x-\frac{21}{16} \log (4 \sin x+7)+\mathrm{c}, \end{aligned} $$

where $\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{c}_1+\frac{21}{16}$

Comparing with the given expression, we get

$$ \mathrm{A}=\frac{3}{4}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{21}{16} $$

$$ \therefore \quad A+B=\frac{33}{16} $$

content:
If $\int \frac{2 x+3}{(x-1)\left(x^2+1\right)} d x$

$$ =\log _e\left\{(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}\left(x^2+1\right)^2\right\}-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+\mathrm{A} $$

where A is an arbitrary constant, then the value of $a$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{2 x+3}{(x-1)\left(x^2+1\right)} \mathrm{d} x \\ & \frac{2 x+3}{(x-1)\left(x^2+1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{x-1}+\frac{\mathrm{B} x+\mathrm{C}}{x^2+1} \\ \therefore \quad & 2 x+3=\mathrm{A}\left(x^2+1\right)+(\mathrm{B} x+\mathrm{C})(x-1) \\ & =(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) x^2+(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{B}) x+(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{C}) \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}=0 \\ & \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{B}=2 \\ & \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{C}=3 \end{array}\\ &\text { On solving, we get }\\ &\mathrm{A}=\frac{5}{2}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{-5}{2}, \mathrm{C}=\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & I=\int \frac{\frac{5}{2}}{x-1} d x+\int \frac{\frac{-5}{2} x-\frac{1}{2}}{x^2+1} d x \\ & =\frac{5}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\frac{5}{2} \int \frac{x}{x^2+1} d x-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+1} d x \\ & =\frac{5}{2} \log (x-1)-\frac{5}{4} \log \left(x^2+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+A \\ & =\log (x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}+\log \left(x^2+1\right)^{\frac{-5}{4}}-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+A \\ & =\log \left[(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}\left(x^2+1\right)^{\frac{-5}{4}}\right]-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+A \\ \therefore \quad & a=\frac{-5}{4} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\int \tan ^4 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{a} \tan ^3 x+\mathrm{b} \tan x+\mathrm{c} x+\mathrm{k}$ (where k is the constant of integration) then the value of $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \tan ^4 x \mathrm{~d} x=\int\left(\tan ^2 x\right)^2 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int\left(\sec ^2 x-1\right)^2 \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & =\int\left(\sec ^4 x-2 \sec ^2 x+1\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int \sec ^4 x \mathrm{~d} x-2 \int \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x+\int 1 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \sec ^2 x \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x-2 \tan x+x \\ & =\int\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x-2 \tan x+x \\ & \quad \text { Put } \tan x=\mathrm{u} \\ & \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{du} \\ & =\int\left(1+\mathrm{u}^2\right) \mathrm{du}-2 \tan x+x \\ & =\mathrm{u}+\frac{\mathrm{u}^3}{3}-2 \tan x+x+\mathrm{k} \\ & =\tan x+\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-2 \tan x+x+\mathrm{k} \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \tan ^3 x-\tan x+x+\mathrm{k} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Comparing with, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \tan ^4 x d x=a \tan ^3 x+b \tan x+c x+k, \text { we get } \\ & a=\frac{1}{3}, b=-1, c=1 \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow a-b+c & =\frac{1}{3}-(-1)+1 \\ & =\frac{1}{3}+1+1 \\ & =\frac{7}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{2 \mathrm{e}^{2 x}+3 \mathrm{e}^x+1} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Let } \mathrm{e}^x=\mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{e}^x}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{d t}{t\left(2 t^2+3 t+1\right)} \\ & =\int \frac{d t}{t(2 t+1)(t+1)} \\ & \text { Let } \frac{1}{t(2 t+1)(t+1)}=\frac{A}{t}+\frac{B}{(2 t+1)}+\frac{C}{t+1} \\ & \Rightarrow 1=A(2 t+1)(t+1)+B t(t+1)+C t(2 t+1)\quad\text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Put $t=-1$ in (i), we get

$$C=1$$

Put $t=\frac{-1}{2}$ in (i), we get

$$B=-4$$

Put $t=0$ in (i), we get

$$A=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \int \frac{d t}{t(2 t+1)(t+1)} \\ & =\int \frac{1}{t} d t-4 \int \frac{1}{2 t+1} d t+\int \frac{1}{t+1} d t \\ & =\log t-\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \log |2 t+1|+\log |t+1|+c \\ & =\log e^x+\log \left|e^x+1\right|-2 \log \left|2 e^x+1\right|+c \\ & =x+\log \left(e^x+1\right)-2 \log \left(2 e^x+1\right)+c \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \cos t \cdot e^t d t \\ & =\cos t \cdot e^t-\int(-\sin t) \cdot e^t d t \\ & =\cos t \cdot e^t+\left[\sin t \cdot e^t-\int \cos t \cdot e^t d t\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\cos t \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}+\sin t \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=\cos t \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}+\sin t \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{x}{2}\left[\cos \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right)+\sin \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right)\right]+\mathrm{c}, \text { where } \mathrm{c}=\frac{\mathrm{c}_1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Let $7-6 x-x^2=\mathrm{t}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I} =-\int(t)^{\frac{-1}{2}} \mathrm{dt}-\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{4}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =-2 \sqrt{7-6 x-x^2}-\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{4}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore & \quad\mathrm{~A} =-2 \text { and } \mathrm{B}=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B}=-3 \end{aligned} $$

$$\text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+2)} \mathrm{d} x$$

Let $\frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+2)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{(x-1)}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{(x-1)^2}+\frac{\mathrm{C}}{(x+2)}$

$$\therefore \quad x=\mathrm{A}(x-1)(x+2)+\mathrm{B}(x+2)+\mathrm{C}(x-1)^2$$

for $x=1$, we get $B=\frac{1}{3}$

for $x=-2$, we get $C=\frac{-2}{9}$

Equating the coefficients of $x^2$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & A+C=0 \\ \therefore \quad & =\frac{2}{9} \\ \therefore \quad & =\frac{2}{9} \int \frac{1}{(x-1)} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{(x-1)^2} d x-\frac{2}{9} \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x \\ & =\frac{2}{9} \log (x-1)-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{(x-1)}-\frac{2}{9} \log (x+2)+c \end{aligned}$$

content: $\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}}=2 \tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{f}(x))+\mathrm{c}$ where $x>0$ and c is a constant of integration, then $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is

explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}}$

Let $\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}=\mathrm{t}$

$\therefore\quad\mathrm{e}^x-1=\mathrm{t}^2$

$\therefore\quad\mathrm{e}^x=\mathrm{t}^2+1$

$\therefore\quad\mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{dx}=2 \mathrm{tdt}$

$\therefore\quad\mathrm{d} x=\frac{2 \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{e}^x} \mathrm{dt}=\frac{2 \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}\quad\text{... [from (i)]}$

$\therefore\quad I=\int \frac{1}{t} \times \frac{2 t}{t^2+1} d t$

$=2 \int \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}$

$=2 \tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c}$

$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}\right)+\mathrm{c}$

$\therefore\quad\mathrm{f}(x)=\sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \frac{x^4+1}{x^4}=t \\ & \text { i.e., } 1+\frac{1}{x^4}=t \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{-4}{x^5} d x=d t \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\frac{-1}{4} \int t^{\frac{-3}{4}} d t \\ & =-t^{\frac{1}{4}}+c \\ & =-\left(\frac{x^4+1}{x^4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}+c \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan t}{1+\tan ^2 t}\right) \sec ^2 t \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\int\left(\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \mathrm{t}) \sec ^2 \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\int 2 \mathrm{t} \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{t} \\ & =2\left[\mathrm{t} \int \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{dt}-\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\int \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{dt}\right) \mathrm{dt}\right]+\mathrm{c} \\ & =2\left[\mathrm{t} \tan \mathrm{t}-\int \tan \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{dt}\right]+\mathrm{c} \\ & =2 \mathrm{t} \tan \mathrm{t}+2 \log |\cos \mathrm{t}|+\mathrm{c} \\ & =2 x \tan ^{-1} x+2 \log \left|\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & =2 x \tan ^{-1} x-\log \left(1+x^2\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If, $\int \frac{d \theta}{\cos ^2 \theta(\tan 2 \theta+\sec 2 \theta)}=\lambda \tan \theta+2 \log _{\mathrm{e}}|\mathrm{f}(\theta)|+\mathrm{c}$ (where c is a constant of integration), then the ordered pair $(\lambda,|f(\theta)|)$ is equal to

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int \frac{d \theta}{\cos ^2 \theta(\tan 2 \theta+\sec 2 \theta)} \\ & =\int \frac{\sec ^2 \theta d \theta}{\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^2 \theta}\right)+\left(\frac{1+\tan ^2 \theta}{1-\tan ^2 \theta}\right)} \\ & =\int \frac{\sec ^2 \theta\left(1-\tan ^2 \theta\right) d \theta}{(1+\tan \theta)^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Put } \tan \theta=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \sec ^2 \theta d \theta=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} & =\int \frac{\left(1-\mathrm{t}^2\right) \mathrm{dt}}{(1+\mathrm{t})^2}=\int \frac{1-\mathrm{t}}{1+\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\int \frac{2-(1+\mathrm{t})}{1+\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =2 \log |1+\mathrm{t}|-\mathrm{t}+\mathrm{C} \\ & =2 \log |1+\tan \theta|-\tan \theta+C \end{aligned} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Comparing with } \lambda \tan \theta+2 \log |f(\theta)|+C \text {, we get }\\ &\therefore \quad \lambda=-1, \mathrm{f}(\theta)=1+\tan \theta \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\quad \int(2 x+4) \sqrt{x-1} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{a}(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}+\mathrm{b}(x-1)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{c}$, (where c is a constant of integration), then the value of $a+b$ is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int(2 x+4) \sqrt{x-1} d x$

Put $x-1=\mathrm{t}^2$

$\mathrm{dx}=2 \mathrm{tdt}$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int\left(2\left(\mathrm{t}^2+1\right)+4\right) \cdot \mathrm{t} \times 2 \mathrm{tdt}$

$=\int\left(2 t^2+2+4\right) 2 t^2 \cdot d t$

$=\int\left(2 t^2+6\right) 2 t^2 d t$

$=\int\left(4 t^4+12 t^2\right) d t$

$=4 \int \mathrm{t}^4 \mathrm{dt}+12 \int \mathrm{t}^2 \mathrm{dt}$

$=4 \frac{\mathrm{t}^5}{5}+12 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{t}^3}{3}+\mathrm{C}$

$=\frac{4}{5} \mathrm{t}^5+4 \mathrm{t}^3+C$

$=\frac{4}{5}(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}+4(x-1)^{\frac{3}{2}}+C$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { But } \int(2 x+4) \sqrt{x-1} d x=a(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}+b(x-1)^{\frac{3}{2}}+C \\ \therefore & a=\frac{4}{5} \text { and } b=4 \\ \therefore & a+b=\frac{4}{5}+4=\frac{24}{5} \end{array}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{x+1} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Let } \sqrt{x}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~d} x=2 \mathrm{t} \mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} 2 \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{dt} \\ &=\int \frac{2 \mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt} \\ &=2 \int \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt} \\ &=2 \int\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}^2+1-1}{\mathrm{t}^2+1}\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ &=2\left[\int \frac{\mathrm{t}^2+1}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}-\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}\right] \\ &=2\left[\mathrm{t}-\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]+\mathrm{c} \\ & \mathrm{I}=2\left[\sqrt{x}-\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right]+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{1+\sin (\log x)}{1+\cos (\log x)} \mathrm{d} x \\ & \text { Put } \log x=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \\ \therefore\quad& \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int\left(\frac{1+\sin t}{1+\cos t}\right) \cdot e^t d t \\ I & =\int\left(\frac{\sin ^2 \frac{t}{2}+\cos ^2 \frac{t}{2}+2 \sin \frac{t}{2} \cos \frac{t}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{t}{2}}\right) e^t d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\tan ^2 \frac{t}{2}+1+2 \tan \frac{t}{2}\right) e^t d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sec ^2 \frac{t}{2}+2 \tan \frac{t}{2}\right) e^t d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \tan \frac{t}{2} \cdot e^t+C \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \ldots\left[\int \mathrm{e}^x\left[\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{e}^x \cdot \mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{c}\right] \\ & =\tan \frac{\mathrm{t}}{2} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \mathrm{I}=\tan \left(\frac{\log x}{2}\right) \cdot x+c \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{x+1}{x\left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)^2} \mathrm{dx}=\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^x(x+1)}{\mathrm{e}^x \cdot x\left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)^2} \mathrm{dx}$

Put $x \cdot \mathrm{e}^x=\mathrm{t}$

$\Rightarrow(x+1) e^x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{d t}{t(1+t)^2} \\ & =\int \frac{1+t-t}{t(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\int \frac{1 d t}{t(1+t)}-\int \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\int \frac{1+t-t}{t(1+t)}-\int \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\int \frac{1}{t} d t-\int \frac{1}{(t+1)} d t-\int \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\log t-\log (1+t)+\frac{1}{(1+t)}+c \\ & =\log x \mathrm{e}^x-\log \left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)+\frac{1}{\left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)}+c \\ \therefore \quad I & =\log \left|\frac{x \mathrm{e}^x}{1+x \mathrm{e}^x}\right|+\frac{1}{1+x \mathrm{e}^x}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

Put $\mathrm{e}^x-1=\mathrm{t}^2$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{dx}=2 \mathrm{tdt}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{dx}=\frac{2 \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}$

$\therefore \quad I=\int \mathrm{t} \cdot \frac{2 \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}=\int \frac{2 \mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}$

$=\int \frac{2\left(\mathrm{t}^2+1\right)-2}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}$

$=2 \int \mathrm{dt}-2 \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1}$

$=2 \mathrm{t}-2 \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}$

$=2 \sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}-2 \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{e}^x-1}+\mathrm{c}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \text { Put } \cos x+\sin x=t \\ & \quad \Rightarrow(-\sin x+\cos x) d x=d t \\ & \therefore \quad I=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{3^2-t^2}} d t \\ & \quad=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{3}\right)+c \\ & \quad=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3}\right)+c \\ & \therefore \quad(a, b)=(1,3) \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int \sec ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^{\frac{4}{3}} x \\ & =\frac{1}{\cos ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \sin ^{\frac{4}{3}} x} d x \\ & =\frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sin ^{\frac{4}{3}} x}{\cos ^{\frac{4}{3}} x}\right) \times \cos ^2 x} \mathrm{dx} \\ & =\int \frac{\sec ^2 x}{(\tan x)^{\frac{4}{3}}} d x \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Put } \tan x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}\\ \therefore\quad &I=\int \frac{d t}{t^{\frac{4}{3}}} d t=-3 t^{\frac{1}{3}}+c=-3(\tan x)^{\frac{-1}{3}}+c \end{aligned}$$

$2 \tan \left(1+\log \tan \frac{x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\frac{1}{4} \tan \left(1+\log \tan \frac{x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\operatorname{cosec} x \mathrm{~d} x}{\cos ^2\left(1+\log \tan \frac{x}{2}\right)} \mathrm{d} x$

Let $1+\log \left(\tan \frac{x}{2}\right)=\mathrm{t}$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. t, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{\tan \frac{x}{2}} \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \operatorname{cosec} x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & =\int \frac{1}{\cos ^2 t} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\int \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} d t \\ & =\tan (\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\tan \left(1+\log \left(\tan \frac{x}{2}\right)\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{array}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=\int \sin \sqrt{x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Let } \sqrt{x}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~d} x=2 \sqrt{x} \mathrm{dt}=2 \mathrm{tdt} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \sin \mathrm{t} \cdot 2 \mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{dt} \\ & =2 \int \sin \mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{tdt} \\ & =2\left[\mathrm{t} \int \sin \mathrm{tdt}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}} \mathrm{t} \int \sin \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{dt}\right] \\ & =2\left[\mathrm{t}(-\cos \mathrm{t})-\int(-\cos \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt}\right] \\ & =2\left[-\mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{t}+\int \cos \mathrm{tdt}\right] \\ & =-2 \mathrm{t} \cos t+2 \sin t+\mathrm{c} \\ & =2 \sin \sqrt{x}-2 \sqrt{x} \cos \sqrt{x}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & f\left(\frac{x-4}{x-2}\right)=2 x+1 \\ & \text { Let } \frac{x-4}{x-2}=y \\ & \frac{x-2-2}{x-2}=y \\ & 1-\frac{2}{x-2}=y \\ & \frac{-2}{x-2}=y-1 \\ & \frac{-2}{y-1}=x-2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{-2}{y-1}+2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x=\frac{-4}{y-1}+4 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x+1=\frac{-4}{y-1}+5 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(y)=\frac{-4}{y-1}+5 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{-4}{x-1}+5 \\ & \therefore \quad \int \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=\int \frac{-4}{x-1}+5\\ & \begin{aligned} & =-4 \log (x-1)+5 x+c \\ & =5 x-4 \log (x-1)+c \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\cos ^3 x \sqrt{2 \sin 2 x}}=(\tan x)^A+C(\tan x)^B+K$, where K is a constant of integration, then the value of $5(A+B+C)$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\cos ^3 x \sqrt{2 \sin 2 x}} \\ & \text { Put } \sin 2 x=\frac{2 \tan x}{1+\tan ^2 x} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\sec ^2 x \cdot \sec ^2 x}{2 \sqrt{x \tan x}} \mathrm{~d} x \end{array}$$

Let $\tan x=\mathrm{t}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \sec ^2 d x=d t \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1+t^2}{\sqrt{t}} d t=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}+t^{\frac{3}{2}}\right) d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}}+\frac{t^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}\right]+k=(\tan \theta)^{\frac{1}{2}}+\frac{1}{5}(\tan \theta)^{\frac{5}{2}}+k \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with $(\tan x)^{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{C}(\tan x)^{\mathrm{B}}+\mathrm{k}$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & A=\frac{1}{2}, B=\frac{5}{2}, C=\frac{1}{5} \\ \therefore \quad & 5(A+B+C)=5\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{5}{2}+\frac{1}{5}\right)=16 \end{aligned}$$

options.content: $\frac{9}{14} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{5}{14 \sqrt{3}} \log \left(\frac{x-\sqrt{3}}{x+\sqrt{3}}\right)+\mathrm{c}$, (where c is constant of integration)
$\frac{9}{7} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{5}{7 \sqrt{3}} \log \left(\frac{x-\sqrt{3}}{x+\sqrt{3}}\right)+c$, (where c is constant of integration)

explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{2 x^2-1}{\left(x^2+4\right)\left(x^2-3\right)} \mathrm{d} x \\ & \frac{2 x^2-1}{\left(x^2+4\right)\left(x^2-3\right)}=\frac{A}{x^2+4}+\frac{B}{x^2-3} \\ \therefore \quad & 2 x^2-1=\mathrm{A}\left(x^2-3\right)+\mathrm{B}\left(x^2+4\right) \\ \therefore \quad & 2 x^2-1=(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) x^2-(3 \mathrm{~A}-4 \mathrm{~B}) \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}=2 \text { and } 3 \mathrm{~A}-4 \mathrm{~B}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~A}=\frac{9}{7} \text { and } \mathrm{B}=\frac{5}{7} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\frac{9}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^2+(2)^2} \mathrm{~d} x+\frac{5}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^2-(\sqrt{3})^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{9}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{5}{7} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \log \left(\frac{x-\sqrt{3}}{x+\sqrt{3}}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{9}{14} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{5}{14 \sqrt{3}} \log \left(\frac{x-\sqrt{3}}{x+\sqrt{3}}\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\quad \int(2 x+4) \sqrt{x-1} d x=a(x-1)^{5 / 2}+b(x-1)^{3 / 2}+c$ where $c$ is a constant of integration, then the value of $(2 a+b)$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } I=\int(2 x+4) \sqrt{x-1} d x \\ & \text { Let } x-1=t \\ \therefore \quad x & =1+t \\ \therefore \quad & d x=d t \\ \therefore \quad I & =\int[2(1+t)+4] \sqrt{t} d t \\ & =\int\left(6 \sqrt{t}+2 t^{\frac{3}{2}}\right) d t \\ & =\frac{6 t^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}+2 \frac{t^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}+c\\ & =4(x-1)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{4}{5}(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}+c \end{array}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Comparing with } \mathrm{a}(x-1)^{\frac{5}{2}}+\mathrm{b}(x-1)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{c} \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & a=\frac{4}{5}, b=4 \\ \therefore\quad& 2 a+b=\frac{8}{5}+4=\frac{28}{5} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\int \frac{x+1}{\sqrt{2 x-1}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{f}(x) \sqrt{2 x-1}+\mathrm{c}$, (where c is a constant of integration), then $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{x+1}{\sqrt{2 x-1}} \mathrm{~d} x$

Put $2 x-1=\mathrm{t}^2 \Rightarrow x+1=\frac{\mathrm{t}^2+3}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{tdt}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{\left(\frac{t^2+3}{2}\right) \mathrm{tdt}}{\mathrm{t}} \\ & =\frac{t^3}{6}+\frac{3}{2} t+c \\ & =\frac{t}{2}\left(\frac{t^2+9}{3}\right)+c \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{2 x-1}}{2}\left(\frac{2 x-1+9}{3}\right)+c \\ & =\sqrt{2 x-1}\left(\frac{x+4}{3}\right)+c \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with $\mathrm{f}(x) \sqrt{2 x-1}+\mathrm{c}$, we get

$$f(x)=\frac{x+4}{3}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \int \frac{\log \sqrt{x}}{3 x} \mathrm{~d} x=\int \frac{\log \mathrm{t}}{3 \mathrm{t}^2}(2 \mathrm{tdt}) \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{\log \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{(\log \mathrm{t})^2}{2}+\mathrm{c}=\frac{(\log \sqrt{x})^2}{3}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{2} \log x\right)^2+c \\ & =\frac{1}{12}(\log x)^2+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int 3^{3^x} \cdot 3^x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Let } 3^x=\mathrm{t} \\ & 3^x \log 3 \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & 3^x \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{1}{\log 3} \mathrm{dt} \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int 3^{\mathrm{t}} \cdot \frac{1}{\log 3} \cdot \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 3} \int 3^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 3} \times \frac{3^{\mathrm{t}}}{\log 3}+\mathrm{c} \\ \mathrm{I} & =\frac{3^{3^x}}{(\log 3)^2}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \log (1+x)^{1+x} \mathrm{~d} x$

$$\mathrm{I}=\int(1+x) \cdot \log (1+x) \mathrm{d} x$$

Put $1+x=\mathrm{t}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{d} x & =\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \mathrm{t} \cdot \log \mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\log \mathrm{t} \int \mathrm{t} \mathrm{dt}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}} \log \mathrm{t} \cdot \int \mathrm{tdt}\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\log \mathrm{t} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}-\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \times \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\log \mathrm{t} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int \mathrm{t} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\log \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{4}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\mathrm{t}^2 \frac{\log \mathrm{t}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{4}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}\left[\log \mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{2}\right]+\mathrm{c} \\ \mathrm{I} & =\frac{(1+x)^2}{2}\left[\log (1+x)-\frac{1}{2}\right]+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: $\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{3-2 \cos 2 x}=\frac{\tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{f}(x))}{\sqrt{5}}+\mathrm{c}$, (where c is a constant of integration), then $f(\pi / 4)$ has the value

$\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$
$\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$

explanation:
Let $I=\int \frac{d x}{3-2 \cos 2 x}$

Put $\tan x=\mathrm{t}$

$$\therefore \quad x=\tan ^{-1} t$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{d} & =\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2} \\ & \cos 2 x=\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \frac{\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}}{3-2\left(\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}\right)} \\ & =\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{3\left(1+\mathrm{t}^2\right)-2\left(1-\mathrm{t}^2\right)} \\ & =\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{3+3 \mathrm{t}^2-2+2 \mathrm{t}^2} \\ & =\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\left(\sqrt{5} t^2\right)^2+(1)^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{5} \mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{5} \tan x+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Comparing with } \frac{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{f}(x)}{\sqrt{5}} \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\sqrt{5} \tan x \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sqrt{5} \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=\sqrt{5} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{p} \sqrt{1-x}\left(3 x^2+4 x+8\right)+\mathrm{c}$$ where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration, then the value of $$p$$ is
explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x}} d x$$

Put $$1-x=\mathrm{t}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{d} x=-\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{(1-t)^2}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot(-d t) \\ & =-\int \frac{1-2 t+t^2}{t^{\frac{1}{2}}} d t \\ & =-\int\left(t^{-\frac{1}{2}}-2 t^{\frac{1}{2}}+t^{\frac{3}{2}}\right) d t \\ & =-\left(2 t^{\frac{1}{2}}-\frac{4}{3} t^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{2}{5} t^{\frac{5}{2}}\right)+c \\ & =t^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(\frac{-30+20 t-6 t^2}{15}\right)+c \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{1-x}\left(\frac{-30+20(1-x)-6(1-x)^2}{15}\right)+c \\ & =\sqrt{1-x}\left(\frac{-6 x^2-8 x-16}{15}\right)+c \\ & =-\frac{2}{15} \sqrt{1-x}\left(3 x^2+4 x+8\right)+c \\ \therefore \quad p & =\frac{-2}{15} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\cot ^2 x-1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~A}} \log |\sec 2 x+\tan 2 x|-\frac{x}{\mathrm{~B}}+\mathrm{c}$$, (where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is constant of integration), then $$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\cot ^2 x-1} & =\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\frac{\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x}{\sin ^2 x}} \\ & =\int \frac{\sin ^2 x}{\cos 2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\frac{1-\cos 2 x}{2}}{\cos 2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int(\sec 2 x-1) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\log |\sec 2 x+\tan 2 x|}{2}-x\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{4} \log |\sec 2 x+\tan 2 x|-\frac{x}{2}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{r} \therefore \quad A=4, B=2 \\ \Rightarrow A+B=6 \end{array}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{\cos \theta}{5+7 \sin \theta-2 \cos ^2 \theta} d \theta=A \log _e|f(\theta)|+c$$ (where $$c$$ is a constant of integration), then $$\frac{f(\theta)}{A}$$ can be

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int \frac{\cos \theta}{5+7 \sin \theta-2 \cos ^2 \theta} d \theta \\ & =\int \frac{\cos \theta}{5+7 \sin \theta-2\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)} d \theta \\ & =\int \frac{\cos \theta}{2 \sin ^2 \theta+7 \sin \theta+3} d \theta \\ & =\int \frac{\cos \theta}{(\sin \theta+3)(2 \sin \theta+1)} d \theta \end{aligned}$$

Put $$\sin \theta=t$$

$$\Rightarrow \cos \theta d \theta=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & I=\int \frac{d t}{(t+3)(2 t+1)} \\ & =\int\left[\frac{2}{5(2 t+1)}-\frac{1}{5(t+3)}\right] d t \\ & =\frac{2}{5} \cdot \frac{\log \mid 2 t+1}{2}-\frac{1}{5} \log |t+3|+c \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \log |2 \sin \theta+1|-\frac{1}{5} \log |\sin \theta+3|+c \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \log \left|\frac{2 \sin \theta+1}{\sin \theta+3}\right|+c \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{5}, \mathrm{f}(\theta)=\frac{2 \sin \theta+1}{\sin \theta+3} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{f}(\theta)}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{5(2 \sin \theta+1)}{\sin \theta+3} \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Put } 1+x^2=t^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x \mathrm{~d} x=2 \mathrm{tdt} \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{tdt} \\ & \therefore \quad \int \frac{x^3 \mathrm{~d} x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}} \\ & =\int \frac{\mathrm{t}^2-1}{\mathrm{t}} \cdot \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{t} \\ & =\int\left(\mathrm{t}^2-1\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{t}^3}{3}-\mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{\left(1+x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{3}-\sqrt{1+x^2}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{3}\left(1+x^2\right) \sqrt{1+x^2}-\sqrt{1+x^2}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \quad a=\frac{1}{3}, b=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 a b=-1 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$f(x)=\int \frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^4+1} \mathrm{~d} x$$, then function decreases in the interval
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\int \frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^4+1} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^4+1} \end{aligned}$$

For $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ to be decreasing,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^4+1}<0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{(x-1)(x-2)}{x^4+1}<0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-1)(x-2)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in(1,2) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{\log \left(t+\sqrt{1+t^2}\right)}{\sqrt{1+t^2}} d t=\frac{1}{2}[g(t)]^2+c$$, (where $$c$$ is a constant of integration), then $$g(2)$$ is
explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{\log \left(t+\sqrt{1+t^2}\right)}{\sqrt{1+t^2}} d t$$

Now assume $$y=\log \left(t+\sqrt{1+t^2}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad d y & =\frac{1}{t+\sqrt{1+t^2}}\left(1+\frac{t}{\sqrt{1+t^2}}\right) d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+t^2}} d t \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int y d y=\frac{y^2}{2}+c \\ & =\frac{\left(\log \left(t+\sqrt{1+t^2}\right)\right)^2}{2}+c \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Thus, } g(t)=\log \left(t+\sqrt{1+t^2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad g(2)=\log (2+\sqrt{5}) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$I=\int \frac{e^x}{e^{4 x}+e^{2 x}+1} d x$$ and $$J=\int \frac{e^{-x}}{e^{-4 x}+e^{-2 x}+1} d x$$, then for any arbitrary constant $$C$$, than the value of $$J-I$$ equals

explanation:
$$\begin{gathered} \text { } \because I=\int \frac{e^x}{e^{4 x}+e^{2 x}+1} d x \text { and } \\ J=\int \frac{e^{3 x}}{1+e^{2 x}+e^{4 x}} d x \\ \therefore \quad J-I=\int \frac{e^{3 x}-e^x}{1+e^{2 x}+e^{4 x}} d x \end{gathered}$$

By substituting $$e^x=u \Rightarrow e^x d x=d u$$

$$\therefore \quad J-I=\int \frac{u^2-1}{1+u^2+u^4} d u=\int \frac{1-\frac{1}{u^2}}{1+\frac{1}{u^2}+u^2} d u$$

By substituting $$u+\frac{1}{u}=t$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(1-\frac{1}{u^2}\right) d u=d t \\ J-I & =\int \frac{d t}{t^2-1} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{t-1}{t+1}\right|+C \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{u^2-u+1}{u^2+u+1}\right|+C \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{e^{2 x}-e^x+1}{e^{2 x}+e^x+1}\right|+C \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int x^5 e^{-4 x^3} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{1}{48} \mathrm{e}^{-4 x^3} \mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration, then $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ is given by
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int x^5 \mathrm{e}^{-4 x^3} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } x^3=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow 3 x^2 \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad I=\frac{1}{3} \int t e^{-4 t} d t=\frac{1}{3}\left(t \cdot \frac{e^{-4 t}}{-4}-\int 1 \cdot \frac{e^{-4 t}}{-4} d t\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{te}^{-4 \mathrm{t}}}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{e}^{-4 \mathrm{t}}}{-4}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{48} \mathrm{e}^{-4 \mathrm{t}}(-4 \mathrm{t}-1)+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{48} e^{-4 x^3}\left(-4 x^3-1\right)+c \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x)=-4 x^3-1 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Put } x & =\tan \theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{d} x=\sec ^2 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) & =\int \frac{\tan ^2 \theta \sec ^2 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}{\sec ^2 \theta(1+\sec \theta)} \\ & =\int \frac{\tan ^2 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}{1+\sec \theta} \\ & =\int \frac{\sin ^2 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}{\cos \theta(1+\cos \theta)} \\ & =\int \frac{1-\cos ^2 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}{\cos \theta(1+\cos \theta)} \\ & =\int \frac{(1-\cos \theta) \mathrm{d} \theta}{\cos \theta} \\ & =\int \sec \theta \mathrm{d} \theta-\int \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & =\log |\sec \theta+\tan \theta|-\theta+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) & =\log \left|x+\sqrt{1+x^2}\right|-\tan ^{-1} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(0) & =\log |0+\sqrt{1+0}|-\tan ^{-1}(0)+\mathrm{c} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 0 & =\log 1-0+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=0 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) & =\log \left|x+\sqrt{1+x^2}\right|-\tan ^{-1} x \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(1) & =\log \left|1+\sqrt{1+1^2}\right|-\tan ^{-1}(1) \\ & =\log (1+\sqrt{2})-\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\tan x=t \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int \frac{1+t^2}{\sqrt{t}} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int t^{\frac{1}{2}} d t+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int t^{\frac{3}{2}} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}}\right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{t^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}\right)+c \\ & =\sqrt{2}\left(\sqrt{\tan x}+\frac{1}{5}(\tan x)^{\frac{5}{2}}\right)+c \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{x^4} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{A}(x)\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)^{\mathrm{m}}+\mathrm{c}$$ for a suitable chosen integer $$\mathrm{m}$$ and a function $$\mathrm{A}(x)$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration, then $$(\mathrm{A}(x))^{\mathrm{m}}$$ equals
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad\mathrm{I} & =\int \frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{x^4} d x \\ & =\int \frac{x \sqrt{\frac{1}{x^2}-1}}{x^4} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\sqrt{\frac{1}{x^2}-1}}{x^3} d x \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\quad\frac{1}{x^2}-1=\mathrm{t}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{-2}{x^3} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^3} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \sqrt{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & \quad=\frac{-1}{2} \times \frac{(\mathrm{t})^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \quad=\frac{-1}{3} \times\left(\frac{1}{x^2}-1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{-1}{3} \times \frac{\left(1-x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\left(x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}+c \\ & =\frac{-1}{3} \times \frac{\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)^3}{x^3} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with $$\mathrm{A}(x)\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)^{\mathrm{m}}+\mathrm{c}$$, we get $$A(x)=\frac{-1}{3 x^3}$$ and $$\mathrm{m}=3$$

$$\therefore \quad(\mathrm{A}(x))^{\mathrm{m}}=\left(\frac{-1}{3 x^3}\right)^3=\frac{-1}{27 x^9}$$

$$\tan \left(\tan \frac{x}{2}\right)+c$$, where c is constant of integration.

explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{\operatorname{cosec} x d x}{\cos ^2\left(1+\log \tan \frac{x}{2}\right)} d x$$

Let $$1+\log \left(\tan \frac{x}{2}\right)=\mathrm{t}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$\mathrm{t}$$, we get

$$\frac{1}{\tan \frac{x}{2}} \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\therefore \quad \operatorname{cosec} x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{1}{\cos ^2 \mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} & =\int \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{t} \\ & =\tan (\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\tan \left(1+\log \left(\tan \frac{x}{2}\right)\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The integral $$\int \frac{\sin ^2 x \cos ^2 x}{\left(\sin ^5 x+\cos ^3 x \sin ^2 x+\sin ^3 x \cos ^2 x+\cos ^5 x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x$$ is equal to

options.content: $$\frac{1}{3\left(1+\tan ^3 x\right)}+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\frac{-1}{3\left(1+\tan ^3 x\right)}+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\frac{1}{1+\cot ^3 x}+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\frac{-1}{1+\cos ^3 x}+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
Let

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{\sin ^2 x \cos ^2 x}{\left(\sin ^5 x+\cos ^3 x \sin ^2 x+\sin ^3 x \cos ^2 x+\cos ^5 x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\sin ^2 x \cos ^2 x}{\left(\sin ^5 x+\sin ^3 x \cos ^2 x+\cos ^3 x \sin ^2 x+\cos ^5 x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\sin ^2 x \cos ^2 x}{\left[\sin ^3 x\left(\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x\right)+\cos ^3 x\left(\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x\right)\right]^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\sin ^2 x \cos ^2 x}{\left(\sin ^3 x+\cos ^3 x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\sec ^2 x \tan ^2 x}{\left(1+\tan ^3 x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

....[Dividing numerator and denominator by $$\cos ^6 x$$ ]

Let $$1+\tan ^3 x=\mathrm{t}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \tan ^2 x \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \tan ^2 x \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~dt} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{-1}{3 \mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{-1}{3\left(1+\tan ^3 x\right)}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{x^2+1}{x\left(x^2-1\right)} d x \\ & \quad=\int \frac{\frac{x^2+1}{x^2}}{\frac{x^2-1}{x}} d x \\ & \quad \text { Let } \mathrm{t}=\frac{x^2-1}{x} \Rightarrow d t=\frac{x^2+1}{x^2} d x \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}=\log (\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c}=\log \left(\frac{x^2-1}{x}\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \cos ^{\frac{3}{5}} x \cdot \sin ^3 x d x=\frac{-1}{m} \cos ^m x+\frac{1}{n} \cos ^n x+c$$, (where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is the constant of integration), then $$(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=$$

options.content: $$\left(\frac{18}{5}, \frac{8}{5}\right)$$
$$\left(\frac{-8}{5}, \frac{18}{5}\right)$$
$$\left(\frac{8}{5}, \frac{18}{5}\right)$$

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad\mathrm{I} & =\int \cos ^{\frac{3}{5}} x \sin ^3 x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \cos ^{\frac{3}{5}} x\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right) \sin x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \cos ^{\frac{3}{5}} x \sin x \mathrm{~d} x-\int \cos ^{\frac{13}{5}} x \sin x \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} \\ & \text { Let } \cos x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow-\sin x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \quad I=-\int \mathrm{t}^{\frac{3}{5}} \mathrm{dt}+\int \mathrm{t}^{\frac{13}{5}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{-1}{\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)} \mathrm{t}^{\frac{8}{5}}+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{18}{5}\right)} \mathrm{t}^{\frac{13}{5}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{-1}{\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)} \cos ^{\frac{8}{5}} x+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{18}{5}\right)} \cos ^{\frac{13}{5}} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { Comparing with } \frac{-1}{\mathrm{~m}} \cos ^{\mathrm{m}} x+\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} \cos ^{\mathrm{n}} x+\mathrm{c}, \text { we get } \mathrm{m} =\frac{8}{5}, \mathrm{n}=\frac{18}{5} \end{aligned}$$

$$2 \log \left(x^2+1\right)+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{I}=\int x \sqrt{\frac{2 \sin \left(x^2+1\right)-\sin 2\left(x^2+1\right)}{2 \sin \left(x^2+1\right)+\sin 2\left(x^2+1\right)}} d x$$

$$=\int x \sqrt{\frac{2 \sin \left(x^2+1\right)-2 \sin \left(x^2+1\right) \cos \left(x^2+1\right)}{2 \sin x\left(x^2+1\right)+2 \sin \left(x^2+1\right) \cos \left(x^2+1\right)}} \mathrm{d} x$$

$$=\int x \sqrt{\frac{1-\cos \left(x^2+1\right)}{1+\cos \left(x^2+1\right)}} \mathrm{d} x$$

$$=\int x \sqrt{\frac{2 \sin ^2\left(\frac{x^2+1}{2}\right)}{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x^2+1}{2}\right)}} d x$$

$$=\int x \tan \left(\frac{x^2+1}{2}\right) d x$$

Let $$\left(\frac{x^2+1}{2}\right)=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \tan \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{t} \\ & =\log (\sec \mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\log \left(\sec \left(\frac{x^2+1}{2}\right)\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{\cos 8 x+1}{\cot 2 x-\tan 2 x} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{A} \cos 8 x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is an arbitrary constant, then the value of $$\mathrm{A}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{\cos 8 x+1}{\cot 2 x-\tan 2 x} d x \\ &=\int \frac{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{8 x}{2}\right)}{\frac{\cos 2 x}{\sin 2 x}-\frac{\sin 2 x}{\cos 2 x}} d x \\ & \ldots\left[\because 1+\cos \theta=2 \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{2 \cos ^2(4 x) \times \sin 2 x \times \cos 2 x}{\cos ^2 2 x-\sin ^2 2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\cos ^2(4 x) \sin (4 x)}{\cos (4 x)} d x \\ & \ldots\left[\begin{array}{c} \because \sin 2 \theta=2 \sin \theta \cos \theta \text { and } \\ \cos 2 \theta=\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta \end{array}\right] \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \int 2 \sin (4 x) \cos (4 x) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int \sin 8 x d x \\ & =\frac{-\cos 8 x}{2 \times 8}+c \\ & =\frac{-\cos 8 x}{16}+c \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with '$$A \cos 8 x+c$$', we get $$A=\frac{-1}{16}$$

Let

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} & =\int(1-\cos x) \cdot \operatorname{cosec}^2 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{[\sin x]^2} \mathrm{~d} x \quad \ldots\left[\because 1-\cos \theta=2 \sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}\right] \\ & =\int \frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{\left[2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}\right]^2} \mathrm{~d} x \quad \ldots\left[\because \sin\theta=2 \sin\frac{\theta}{2}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\right]\\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\tan \frac{x}{2}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $$\log x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. t, we get $$\mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \sin (\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\sin t \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\int \cos \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\sin \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\left[\cos \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}+\int \sin \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}\right] \\ & =\sin \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\cos \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\mathrm{I}\\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=x \sin (\log x)-x \cos (\log x)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{x}{2}(\sin (\log x)-\cos (\log x))+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^x(1+x)}{\cos ^2\left(\mathrm{e}^x \cdot x\right)} \mathrm{d} x$$

Put $$\mathrm{e}^x \cdot x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^x(x+1) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\cos ^2 \mathrm{t}}$$

$$\quad=\int \sec ^2 \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{t}$$

$$\quad=\tan \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\tan \left(x \cdot \mathrm{e}^x\right)+\mathrm{c}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x \sqrt{1-x^3}}=\mathrm{k} \log \left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^3}-1}{\sqrt{1-x^3}+1}\right)+\mathrm{c}$$, (where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration), then value of $$\mathrm{k}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x \sqrt{1-x^3}} \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{x^2 \mathrm{~d} x}{x^3 \sqrt{1-x^3}} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} \\ & \begin{array}{l} \text { Put } 1-x^3=\mathrm{t}^2 \\ \Rightarrow-3 x^2 \mathrm{~d} x=2 \mathrm{tdt} \\ \Rightarrow x^2 \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{-2 \mathrm{tdt}}{3} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \frac{-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{tdt}}{3}\right)}{\left(1-\mathrm{t}^2\right) \mathrm{t}} \\ & =-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{1}{1-\mathrm{t}^2} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2-1} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{2} \cdot \log \left|\frac{\mathrm{t}-1}{\mathrm{t}+1}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \log \left|\frac{\sqrt{1-x^3}-1}{\sqrt{1-x^3}+1}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{k} & =\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\log (\cot x)}{\sin 2 x} \mathrm{~d} x$$

Put $$\log (\cot x)=\mathrm{t}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{\cot x} \cdot\left(-\operatorname{cosec}^2 x\right)\right] \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{-\sin x}{\sin ^2 x \cdot \cos x}\right] \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sin 2 x}=\frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=\int \mathrm{t} \cdot\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{2}\right) \\ & =\frac{-1}{4} \mathrm{t}^2+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{-1}{4}[\log (\cot x)]^2+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let ,$$\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{1}{x^2\left(x^4+1\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}} \mathrm{~d} x=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x^5\left(1+\frac{1}{x^4}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}}$$

Put $$1+\frac{1}{x^4}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{-4}{x^5} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & =-\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d t}{t^{\frac{3}{4}}} \\ & =-\frac{1}{4} \times 4 t^{\frac{1}{4}}+c=-t^{\frac{1}{4}}+c \\ & =-\left(1+\frac{1}{x^4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}+c=-\left(\frac{x^4+1}{x^4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}+c \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \frac{5 \tan x}{\tan x-2} \mathrm{~d} x=x+\mathrm{a} \log |\sin x-2 \cos x|+\mathrm{c},$$ (where $$c$$ is a constant of integration), then the value of $$a$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \frac{5 \tan x}{\tan x-2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{5 \sin x}{\sin x-2 \cos x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Let $$5 \sin x=\mathrm{A}(\sin x-2 \cos x) +\mathrm{B} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}(\sin x-2 \cos x)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 5 \sin x=\mathrm{A}(\sin x-2 \cos x)+\mathrm{B}(\cos x+2 \sin x) \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B}=5 \text { and }-2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B}=0 \end{aligned}$$

Solving these equations, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{A} & =1, \mathrm{~B}=2 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int 1 \mathrm{~d} x+2 \int \frac{\cos x+2 \sin x}{\sin x-2 \cos x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =x+2 \log |\sin x-2 \cos x|+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

But $$\int \frac{5 \tan x}{\tan x-2} \mathrm{~d} x=x+\operatorname{alog}|\sin x-2 \cos x|+\mathrm{c}$$

Comparing, we get $$\mathrm{a}=2$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\left(x^2-1\right) \mathrm{d} x}{x^3 \sqrt{2 x^4-2 x^2+1}} \\ & =\int \frac{\left(x^2-1\right) \mathrm{d} x}{x^3 \cdot x^2 \sqrt{\left(2-\frac{2}{x^2}+\frac{1}{x^4}\right)}} \\ & =\int \frac{\left(\frac{x^2-1}{x^5}\right) \mathrm{d} x}{\sqrt{2-\frac{2}{x^2}+\frac{1}{x^4}}} \\ & \text { Put } 2-\frac{2}{x^2}+\frac{1}{x^4}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{4}{x^3}-\frac{4}{x^5}\right) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^2-1}{x^5} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad I = \int {{{{{dt} \over 4}} \over {\sqrt t }}} \\ & =\frac{1}{4} \int \mathrm{t}^{\frac{-1}{2}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}}+c \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2-\frac{2}{x^2}+\frac{1}{x^4}}+\mathrm{c} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content:
If $$\int \sqrt{\frac{x-7}{x-9}} d x=A \sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}+\log \left|(x-8)+\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}\right|+c,$$

(where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration) then $$\mathrm{A}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int \sqrt{\frac{x-7}{x-9}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \sqrt{\frac{(x-7)(x-7)}{(x-9)(x-7)}} \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int \frac{x-7}{\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let }(x-7)=A\left[\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^2-16 x+63\right)\right]+B \\ \therefore & x-7=A(2 x-16)+B \\ \therefore & x-7=2 A x-16 A+B \\ \therefore & A=\frac{1}{2}, B=1 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\frac{1}{2}(2 x-16)+1}{\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 x-16}{\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}} \mathrm{~d} x+\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}+\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{(x-8)^2-(1)^2}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text {... }\left[\int \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)}{\sqrt{\mathrm{f}(x)}} \mathrm{d} x=2 \sqrt{\mathrm{f}(x)}+\mathrm{c}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}+\log \left|x-8+\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { But, } \int \sqrt{\frac{x-7}{x-9}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{A} \sqrt{x^2-16 x+63} \\ & +\log \left|(x-8)+\sqrt{x^2-16 x+63}\right|+c \\ \end{aligned}$$

Comparing, we get

$$\mathrm{A}=1$$

explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sin x+\cos x}$$

Put $$\tan \frac{x}{2}=\mathrm{t}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x=2 \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{d} x=\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2} \mathrm{dt} \end{aligned}$$

and $$\cos x=\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}, \sin x=\frac{2 t}{1+t^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{\frac{2}{1+t^2}}{\frac{2 t}{1+t^2}+\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}} d t \\ & =\int \frac{2}{2 t+1-t^2} d t \\ & =-2 \int \frac{1}{t^2-2 t-1} d t \\ & =-2 \int \frac{1}{t^2-2 t+1-1-1} \\ & =-2 \int \frac{1}{(t-1)^2-(\sqrt{2})^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =-2 \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{\mathrm{t}-1-\sqrt{2}}{\mathrm{t}-1+\sqrt{2}}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{\tan \frac{x}{2}-1-\sqrt{2}}{\tan \frac{x}{2}-1+\sqrt{2}}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{\tan \frac{x}{2}-(\sqrt{2}+1)}{\tan \frac{x}{2}+\sqrt{2}-1}\right|+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

Put $$1+\frac{1}{x^4}=t \Rightarrow \frac{-4}{x^5} d x=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =-\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^{\frac{3}{4}}}=-\frac{1}{4} \times 4 \mathrm{t}^{\frac{1}{4}}+\mathrm{c}=-\mathrm{t}^{\frac{1}{4}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =-\left(1+\frac{1}{x^4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{-\left(x^4+1\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}}{x}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{\sin x}{3+4 \cos ^2 x} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{A} \tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{~B} \cos x)+\mathrm{c}$$, (where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration), then the value of $$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{aligned} \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\sin x}{3+4 \cos ^2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ \text { Put } \cos x=\mathrm{t} \\ -\sin x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad \sin x \mathrm{~d} x=-\mathrm{dt}\\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{3+4 \mathrm{t}^2} \\ =-1 \int \frac{1}{4 \mathrm{t}^2+3} \\ =-1 \int \frac{1}{(2 \mathrm{t})^2+(\sqrt{3})^2} \\ =-1 \times \frac{1}{2 \times \sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{t}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ \mathrm{I} =\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cos x}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ \text { But } \int \frac{\sin x}{3+4 \cos ^2 x} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{A} \tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{~B} \cos x)+\mathrm{c} \\ \text { Comparing above equations, we get } \\ \text { A } =\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{3}}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \therefore \text { A } +\mathrm{B}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{3}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{array}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int(\sqrt{\tan x}+\sqrt{\cot x}) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int\left(\sqrt{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}+\sqrt{\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}}\right) d x \\ & \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin x \cos x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Let } \sin x-\cos x=\mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad(\sin x+\cos x) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \text { Consider, } \sin x-\cos x=\mathrm{t} \\ & \text { Squaring on both sides, we get } \\ & 1-2 \sin t \cdot \cos t=t^2 \\ & 1-t^2=2 \sin t \cdot \cos t \\ & \therefore \quad \sin t \cdot \cos t=\frac{1-t^2}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\frac{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^2}}{\sqrt{2}}} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{2} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^2}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{2} \sin ^{-1}(\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{2} \sin ^{-1}(\sin x-\cos x)+\mathrm{c} \\ \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\tan x+\tan \alpha}{\tan x-\tan \alpha} \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha}}{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}-\frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha}} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\sin x \cos \alpha+\sin \alpha \cos x}{\sin x \cos \alpha-\sin \alpha \cos x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int \frac{\sin (x+\alpha)}{\sin (x-\alpha)} d x \\ & \text { Let } x-\alpha=\mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad I=\frac{\sin (t+2 \alpha)}{\sin t} \\ & =\int \frac{\sin (t) \cos 2 \alpha+\cos (t) \sin 2 \alpha}{\sin (t)} d x \\ & =\cos 2 \alpha \int 1 d t+\sin 2 \alpha \int \cot (\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\cos 2 \alpha \cdot \mathrm{t}+\sin 2 \alpha \cdot \log |\sin (\mathrm{t})|+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=(x-\alpha) \cos 2 \alpha+\log |\sin (x-\alpha)| \sin 2 \alpha+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { But } \int \frac{\tan x+\tan \alpha}{\tan x-\tan \alpha} \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{A}(x) \cos 2 \alpha+\mathrm{B}(x) \sin 2 \alpha+\mathrm{c} \quad \text{.... [Given]}\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}(x)=x-\alpha, \mathrm{B}(x)=\log |\sin (x-\alpha)|+\mathrm{c} \\ \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{x+1}{x\left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x=\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\prime}(x+1)}{\mathrm{e}^x \cdot x\left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Let } x \cdot \mathrm{e}^x=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1) \mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad I=\int \frac{d t}{t(1+t)^2} \\ & =\int \frac{1+t-t}{t(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\int \frac{1 d t}{t(1+t)}-\int \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\int \frac{1+t-t}{t(1+t)}-\int \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\int \frac{1}{t} d t-\int \frac{1}{(t+1)} d t-\int \frac{1}{(1+t)^2} d t \\ & =\log \mathrm{t}-\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{(\mathrm{t}+1)}-\int \frac{1}{(1+\mathrm{t})^2} d \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { Let } y=1+\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{d} y=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\log \mathrm{t}-\int \frac{1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y-\int \frac{1}{y^2} \mathrm{~d} y+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\log \mathrm{t}-\log y+\frac{1}{y}+\mathrm{c} \\ & =\log t-\log (1+t)+\frac{1}{(1+t)}+c \\ & =\log x \mathrm{e}^x-\log \left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)+\frac{1}{\left(1+x \mathrm{e}^x\right)}+c \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\log \left|\frac{x \mathrm{e}^x}{1+x \mathrm{e}^x}\right|+\frac{1}{1+x \mathrm{e}^x}+\mathrm{c} \\ \end{aligned}$$

explanation:

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Put } \cos x=\mathrm{t} \\ & -\sin x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & I=\int \frac{t^2-2}{2 t^2-1} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 t^2-4}{2 t^2-1} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\int \frac{2 t^2-1}{2 t^2-1} d t-\int \frac{3}{2 t^2-1} d t\right] \\ & I=\frac{1}{2} t-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{\sqrt{2} t-1}{\sqrt{2} t+1}\right|+c \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \cos x-\frac{3}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos x-1}{\sqrt{2} \cos x+1}\right|+c \\ & \therefore \quad P=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } Q=\frac{-3}{4 \sqrt{2}} \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{1}{\sin t \cdot \sin (a+t)} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\sin a} \int \frac{\sin a}{\sin t \cdot \sin (a+t)} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\sin a} \int \frac{\sin ((a+t)-t)}{\sin (a+t) \cdot \sin t} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\sin a}\left[\int \frac{\sin (a+t) \cos t}{\sin (a+t) \sin t} d t-\int \frac{\sin t \cos (a+t)}{\sin (a+t) \sin t} d t\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{\sin a}\left[\int \cot t d t-\int \cot (a+t) d t\right] \\ & =\operatorname{cosec} a[\log |\sin t|-\log |\sin (a+t)|]+c \\ & =\operatorname{cosec} a\left[\log \mid \frac{\sin t}{\sin (a+t) \mid}\right]+c \\ & =\operatorname{cosec} a[\log (\sin (x-a) \cdot \operatorname{cosec} x)]+c \end{aligned}$$

content:
$$\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)(x-2)^2} \mathrm{~d} x=$$

(where $$C$$ is a constant of integration)
explanation:
To solve the integral $$\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)(x-2)^2} \, dx$$, we can use partial fraction decomposition. We want to express the integrand as a sum of fractions of the form:

$$\frac{3x - 2}{(x+1)(x-2)^2} = \frac{A}{x+1} + \frac{B}{x-2} + \frac{C}{(x-2)^2}$$

For this, we need to find the constants $ A $, $ B $, and $ C $. Multiplying both sides by the common denominator $(x+1)(x-2)^2$, we get:

$$3x - 2 = A(x-2)^2 + B(x+1)(x-2) + C(x+1)$$

To find $ A $, $ B $, and $ C $, we can equate coefficients or plug in convenient values for $ x $ that simplify the equation:

Let's plug in $ x = 2 $, the root of the denominator, to solve for $ C $:

$$3(2) - 2 = A(0) + B(2+1)(0) + C(2+1)$$

$$4 = 3C$$

$$C = \frac{4}{3}$$

Next, let's plug in $ x = -1 $, the other root:

$$3(-1) - 2 = A(-1-2)^2 + B(0) + C(0)$$

$$-3 - 2 = A(9)$$

$$-5 = 9A$$

$$A = \frac{-5}{9}$$

To find $ B $, we can either equate coefficients for the $ x $ term or choose another convenient value for $ x $. Let's use $ x = 0 $ to have only $ B $ term remaining:

$$3(0) - 2 = A(0-2)^2 + B(0+1)(0-2) + C(0+1)$$

$$-2 = A(4) - 2B + C$$

Inserting $ A $ and $ C $ we get:

$$-2 = \left(\frac{-5}{9}\right)(4) - 2B + \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)$$

Now, simplify and solve for $ B $:

$$-2 = \frac{-20}{9} - 2B + \frac{4}{3}$$

Combining the fractions gives us:

$$-2 = -\frac{20}{9} + \frac{12}{9} - 2B$$

$$-2 = -\frac{8}{9} - 2B$$

Add $\frac{8}{9}$ to both sides:

$$-2 + \frac{8}{9} = -2B$$

$$-18/9 + 8/9 = -2B$$

$$-10/9 = -2B$$

$$B = \frac{5}{9}$$

Now, the integrand can be rewritten as:

$$\frac{3x - 2}{(x+1)(x-2)^2} = \frac{-5/9}{x+1} + \frac{5/9}{x-2} + \frac{4/3}{(x-2)^2}$$

Integrating term by term:

$$\int \frac{-5/9}{x+1} \, dx + \int \frac{5/9}{x-2} \, dx + \int \frac{4/3}{(x-2)^2} \, dx$$

The integral of each fraction can be calculated as follows:

$$\frac{-5}{9}\ln|x+1| + \frac{5}{9}\ln|x-2| - \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{x-2} + C$$

So the answer to the integral is Option A:

$$\frac{-5}{9} \ln(x+1) + \frac{5}{9}\ln(x-2) - \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{(x-2)} + C$$

Putting $$\frac{x}{2}=\theta$$, i.e., $$\mathrm{d} x=2 \mathrm{~d} \theta$$

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{\sin 5 \theta}{\sin \theta} \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & =\int \frac{\sin \theta \cdot(2 \cos 2 \theta+2 \cos 4 \theta+1)}{\sin \theta} \cdot 2 \mathrm{~d} \theta \\ & =2 \cdot \int(1+2 \cos 2 \theta+2 \cos 4 \theta) d \theta \\ & =2\left[\theta+2\left(\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2}\right)+2\left(\frac{\sin 4 \theta}{4}\right)\right]+c, \ldots \boldsymbol{x}=2 \theta \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2 \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta+\sin 4 \theta+c \\ & =x+2 \sin x+\sin 2 x+c \end{aligned}$$

Note: None of the given option matches the answer.

content: $$\text { If } \int e^{x^2} \cdot x^3 \mathrm{~d} x=e^{x^2} \cdot[f(x)+C]$$ (where $$C$$ is a constant of integration.) and $$f(1)=0$$, then value of $$f(2)$$ will be
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int e^{x^2} \cdot x^3 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int x^2 \cdot e^{x^2} \cdot(2 x) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int\left(t \cdot e^t\right) \mathrm{d} t, \quad \ldots t=x^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[t\left(e^t\right)-\int\left(e^t\right)(1) \mathrm{d} t\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}(t-1) \cdot e^t \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2-1\right) \cdot e^{x^2} \\ & =e^{x^2} \cdot[f(x)-c] \quad \text{... (Given)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore f(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2-1\right)+c \\ & \because f(1)=0 \quad \therefore \frac{1}{2}(0)+c=0 \quad \therefore c=0 \\ & \therefore f(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2-1\right)+0 \\ & \therefore f(2)=\frac{1}{2}(4-1)=\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $$\quad I=\int \cos ^3 x e^{\log (\sin x)^2} d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int \cos ^3 x(\sin x)^2 d x=\int \cos ^2 x(\sin x)^2 \cos x d x \\ & =\int\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right)\left(\sin ^2 x\right) \cos x d x=\int\left(\sin ^2 x-\sin ^4 x\right) \cos x d x \end{aligned}$$

Put $$\sin x=t \Rightarrow \cos x d x=d t$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Put } \sin x=t \Rightarrow \cos x d x=d t . \\ & \therefore I=\int\left(t^2-t^4\right) d t=\frac{t^3}{3}-\frac{t^5}{5}+c=\frac{\sin ^3 x}{3}-\frac{\sin ^5 x}{5}+c \end{aligned}$$

options.content: $$\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}+1}+\mathrm{c}$$

explanation:
Let

$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int \frac{d x}{e^x+e^{-x}+2} \\ & =\int \frac{d x}{e^x+\frac{1}{e^x}+2}=\int \frac{e^x d x}{e^{2 x}+2 e^x+1}=\int \frac{e^x}{\left(e^x+1\right)^2} d x \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Let $$\quad I=\int \frac{1}{\cos x+\sqrt{3} \sin x} d x$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by 2 , we get

$$=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin x\right)}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)}=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\pi}{12}\right)\right|+C$$

To solve the integral $$\int {{e^x}\left( {{{x - 1} \over {{x^2}}}} \right)dx} $$, let's first simplify the integrand.

Rewrite the integrand as follows:

$$ \int {e^x \left( \frac{x - 1}{x^2} \right) dx} = \int {e^x \left( \frac{x}{x^2} - \frac{1}{x^2} \right) dx} = \int {e^x \left( \frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{x^2} \right) dx} $$

So, the integral becomes:

$$ \int {e^x \left( \frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{x^2} \right) dx} = \int {e^x \frac{1}{x} dx} - \int {e^x \frac{1}{x^2} dx} $$

We need to evaluate each term separately. Let's start with the first term:

$$ \int {e^x \frac{1}{x} dx} $$

Applying integration by parts, let:

$$ u = \frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow du = -\frac{1}{x^2} dx $$

$$ dv = e^x dx \Rightarrow v = e^x $$

Using the integration by parts formula $$\int u dv = uv - \int v du$$:

$$ \int {e^x \frac{1}{x} dx} = \frac{e^x}{x} - \int {e^x \left(-\frac{1}{x^2} \right) dx} $$

Note that the second term inside the integration is the same as the second term we need to evaluate originally. So we can write:

$$ \int {e^x \frac{1}{x} dx} = \frac{e^x}{x} + \int {e^x \frac{1}{x^2} dx} $$

Now, notice that:

$$ \int {e^x \frac{1}{x^2} dx} $$

can cancel out the $$ \int {e^x \frac{1}{x^2} dx} $$ from earlier, leaving us with:

$$ \int {e^x \left( \frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{x^2} \right) dx} = \frac{e^x}{x} $$

Thus, the solution to the integral is:

$$ \int {{e^x}\left( {{{x - 1} \over {{x^2}}}} \right)dx = \frac{{e^x}}{x} + c } $$

The correct answer is:

Option D: $$ \frac{{e^x}}{x} + c $$

Let $$I=\int \frac{1+x^2}{1+x^4} d x$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $$\mathrm{x}^2$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)}{\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)} d x=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^2+2} d x \\ & \text { Put } x-\frac{1}{x}=t \Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right) d x=d t \\ & \therefore I=\int \frac{d t}{t^2+2}=\int \frac{d t}{(t)^2+(\sqrt{2})^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{(\mathrm{t})}{\sqrt{2}}\right]+\mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)}{\sqrt{2}}\right]+c\quad ...... (2) \\ & \end{aligned}$$

From (1) and (2), $$f(x)=x-\frac{1}{x}$$

options.content: $$x \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c$$
$$\log (x+\sin x)+c$$
$$\cot \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c$$
$$\log (1+\cos x)+c$$

explanation: Let $$\begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{x+\sin x}{1+\cos x} d x=\int \frac{x+\sin x}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}} d x \\ & \quad=\int \frac{x}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}} d x+\int \frac{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}} d x=\frac{1}{2} \int x \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2} d x+\int \tan \frac{x}{2} d x \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{2}\left[x \tan \frac{x}{2}(2)-\int 2 \tan \frac{x}{2} d x\right]-2 \log \left|\cos \frac{x}{2}\right|+c \\ & \quad=x \tan \frac{x}{2}+2 \log \left|\cos \frac{x}{2}\right|-2 \log \left|\cos \frac{x}{2}\right|+c \quad=x \tan \frac{x}{2}+c \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & I=\int \sin ^{-1} x d x \\ & I=\sin ^{-1} x \int d x-\int \frac{d\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)}{d x} \int d x \cdot d x+C \\ & I=x \sin ^{-1} x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \times x d x+C \\ & I=x \sin ^{-1} x-\int \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}} d x+C \\ & I=x \sin ^{-1} x+\sqrt{1-x^2}+C \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

options.content: $x(\log x)^2+c$

explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int \log x(\log x+2) d x \\ I & =\int(\log x)^2 d x+\int 2 \log x d x \\ I & =(\log x)^2 \int d x-\int\left(\frac{d(\log x)^2}{d x} \int d x\right) d x +\int 2 \log x d x+C \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & I=x(\log x)^2-\int \frac{2 \log x}{x} \cdot x d x+\int 2 \log x d x+c \\ & I=x(\log x)^2-\int 2 \log x d x+\int 2 \log x d x+c \\ & I=(x \log x)^2+c \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{d x}{x^2+4 x+13} \\ & I=\int \frac{d x}{x^2+4 x+4+9}=\int \frac{d x}{(x+2)^2+(3)^2} \\ & I=\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+2}{3}\right)+C \end{aligned}$$

options.content: $$\frac{x}{(1+\log x)}+c$$

explanation:
$$I=\int\left(\frac{\log x-1}{1+(\log x)^2}\right)^2 d x$$

$$\text { Put, } \quad \log x=t \Rightarrow x=e^t \Rightarrow d x=e^t d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{(t-1)^2}{\left(1+t^2\right)^2} e^t d t \\ I & =\int e^t\left(\frac{1+t^2-2 t}{\left(1+t^2\right)^2}\right) d t \\ I & =\int e^t\left(\frac{1}{1+t^2}-\frac{2 t}{\left(1+t^2\right)^2}\right) d t \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Here, } f(t)=\frac{1}{1+t^2} \text { or } f^{\prime}(t)=\frac{-2 t}{\left(1+t^2\right)^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I=\frac{e^t}{1+t^2}+ & c \\ & {\left[\because \int e^x\left(f(x)+f^{\prime}(x) d x=e^x f(x)+c\right]\right.} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x}{1+(\log x)^2}+c$$
